overview: A cloud-native URL shortener with a React frontend (web-app), a Go backend API (service) for link management and redirection, and a scheduled cleanup task. The frontend calls the backend API to create and view links. The cleanup job periodically removes stale links based on last-access time.
requirements: Users can submit a long URL via web UI and receive a short link
Users can submit a long URL via REST API and receive JSON with short link
Visiting a short link redirects to the original long URL
Short links track click count and last access time
Automated deletion of links not accessed within a configurable period
Users can view basic statistics (click count) for their short links
All components expose /health endpoints for readiness checks
Cleanup job runs on a schedule (e.g. daily) to prune stale links

components.name: url-shortener-ui
components.openAPISpec:
openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  title: URL Shortener UI
  version: 1.0.0
  description: Frontend web application for URL shortener
paths:
  /health:
    get:
      summary: Health check
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Service is healthy
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  status:
                    type: string
                    example: ok
components.componentAgentInstructions: Implement a React single-page application in TypeScript. Use Create React App or Vite as the build tool. The app has two main views: (1) a form to submit a long URL and display the resulting short link with a copy button, and (2) a view to display basic stats (click count) for a short link. On form submit, POST to /api/shorten on the backend (url-shortener-api) with JSON body {"url":"https://example.com"}. Display the returned short link prominently. For stats, GET /api/stats/:shortCode from the backend and show click count. Include a /health endpoint that returns {"status":"ok"} with 200 status. Use environment variable for backend API base URL (default http://localhost:9090). Dockerfile: multi-stage build with node:18 for build, nginx:alpine to serve static files. Copy build output to /usr/share/nginx/html. Expose port 80. Include nginx config for SPA routing (fallback to index.html).

components.name: url-shortener-api
components.openAPISpec:
openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  title: URL Shortener API
  version: 1.0.0
  description: Backend service for creating and resolving short links
paths:
  /health:
    get:
      summary: Health check
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Service is healthy
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  status:
                    type: string
                    example: ok
  /api/shorten:
    post:
      summary: Create a short link
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              type: object
              properties:
                url:
                  type: string
                  format: uri
                  example: https://example.com/very/long/path
              required:
                - url
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Short link created
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  shortCode:
                    type: string
                    example: abc123
                  shortUrl:
                    type: string
                    example: http://localhost:9090/abc123
                  originalUrl:
                    type: string
                    example: https://example.com/very/long/path
        '400':
          description: Invalid URL
  /api/stats/{shortCode}:
    get:
      summary: Get click statistics for a short link
      parameters:
        - name: shortCode
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Statistics retrieved
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  shortCode:
                    type: string
                    example: abc123
                  originalUrl:
                    type: string
                    example: https://example.com/very/long/path
                  clickCount:
                    type: integer
                    example: 42
                  lastAccessed:
                    type: string
                    format: date-time
                    example: 2023-10-15T14:30:00Z
        '404':
          description: Short code not found
  /{shortCode}:
    get:
      summary: Redirect to original URL
      parameters:
        - name: shortCode
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
      responses:
        '302':
          description: Redirect to original URL
          headers:
            Location:
              schema:
                type: string
                example: https://example.com/very/long/path
        '404':
          description: Short code not found
components.componentAgentInstructions: Implement a Go HTTP service using net/http. Listen on port 9090. Use an in-memory map (sync.Map or mutex-protected map[string]*Link) to store links. Each Link struct has: OriginalURL (string), ShortCode (string), ClickCount (int64), CreatedAt (time.Time), LastAccessed (time.Time). POST /api/shorten: parse JSON body, validate URL format, generate a random 6-character alphanumeric shortCode (check for collisions), store the link, return JSON with shortCode, shortUrl (constructed as http://HOST:9090/{shortCode}), and originalUrl. GET /{shortCode}: lookup the shortCode, increment ClickCount, update LastAccessed to time.Now(), respond with 302 redirect to OriginalURL. If not found, return 404. GET /api/stats/{shortCode}: lookup and return JSON with shortCode, originalUrl, clickCount, lastAccessed in ISO8601 format. If not found, return 404. GET /health: return {"status":"ok"} with 200. Use CORS middleware to allow requests from the frontend. Dockerfile: multi-stage build with golang:1.21 for build, scratch or alpine for runtime. Copy binary and expose port 9090. Set environment variable PORT=9090. Handle graceful shutdown on SIGTERM/SIGINT.

components.name: link-cleanup-job
components.openAPISpec:
openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  title: Link Cleanup Job
  version: 1.0.0
  description: Scheduled task to delete stale short links
paths:
  /health:
    get:
      summary: Health check
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Service is healthy
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  status:
                    type: string
                    example: ok
components.componentAgentInstructions: Implement a Go command-line application that runs as a Kubernetes CronJob. The job calls the url-shortener-api backend to identify and delete stale links. Environment variable API_BASE_URL points to the backend (default http://url-shortener-api:9090). Environment variable STALE_DAYS defines the threshold (default 30 days). On execution: (1) GET /api/stats from a new endpoint on the backend that lists all links with their lastAccessed timestamps (you may need to extend the backend API or use a shared database). For simplicity, assume the backend exposes GET /api/admin/links returning JSON array of all links. (2) For each link where lastAccessed is older than STALE_DAYS, send DELETE /api/admin/links/{shortCode} to the backend. (3) Log the number of links deleted. Exit with code 0 on success. Dockerfile: multi-stage build with golang:1.21, copy binary to scratch or alpine. The binary is the entrypoint. No long-running HTTP server. For the /health endpoint requirement, include a minimal HTTP server on port 8080 that responds to GET /health with {"status":"ok"}, but the main logic is the cleanup task. Use a goroutine for the HTTP server and run cleanup in main, or structure as a CLI tool where health is optional. Since this is a CronJob, Kubernetes won't query /health during execution, so you may omit the HTTP server and just implement the cleanup logic. If required, add a simple HTTP server that starts, runs cleanup, then exits.
